overview: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) automatically distributes IPv4 configuration (address, subnet mask, default gateway, DNS servers) to hosts, removing the need for manual configuration on every client.
definitions: DORA: the four-message sequence — Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledgment.
DHCP Relay: a router feature that forwards DHCP broadcasts to a server on a different subnet.
DHCP Pool: a named block of configuration (network, exclusions, options) a router hands out as a DHCP server.
core_concepts: DORA process: client broadcasts Discover, server replies Offer, client broadcasts Request (to accept, and to signal other servers it declined their offer), server replies Ack.
DHCP Relay is required whenever the DHCP server sits in a different subnet than the client — the relay device converts the client's broadcast into a unicast aimed at the server.
Exclusions reserve addresses (e.g. for static devices) so the server never offers them dynamically.
protocol_operation: Discover: client broadcasts (255.255.255.255) looking for any DHCP server.
Offer: a server proposes an address/lease.
Request: client broadcasts acceptance of one offer (also tells other servers their offer was not used).
Acknowledgment: server confirms the lease is bound to the client.
packet_flow: Client (no IP) -> broadcasts DHCPDISCOVER -> DHCP server(s) reply DHCPOFFER -> client broadcasts DHCPREQUEST for the chosen offer -> server sends DHCPACK -> client configures its interface with the offered address, mask, gateway, and DNS.

config_commands.purpose: Exclude a range of addresses from a DHCP pool (e.g. static devices).
Define the subnet the pool serves (inside 'ip dhcp pool' mode).
Configure DHCP Relay on the interface facing the client, forwarding broadcasts to the specified server.
Configure an interface to obtain its own IPv4 address via DHCP (client mode).

verification_commands.purpose: Show leases currently handed out by the local router acting as DHCP server.

show_commands.purpose: Primary verification of active DHCP leases.

cli_examples.title: Host-side verification commands
cli_examples.code:
Windows: ipconfig /all
Mac/Linux: ifconfig
Newer Linux: ip address ; ip route
cli_examples.explanation: Used to confirm what IPv4 configuration a client actually received from DHCP.

config_examples.title: Router as DHCP server with relay on a remote subnet
config_examples.code:
ip dhcp excluded-address 10.1.1.1 10.1.1.10
ip dhcp pool LAN1
 network 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0
 default-router 10.1.1.1
 dns-server 8.8.8.8
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip helper-address 10.1.1.1

troubleshooting.symptom: Clients get no IP address at all.
troubleshooting.cause: DHCP Snooping enabled on the switch but the server-facing port is not trusted.
troubleshooting.fix: Mark the server-facing port with 'ip dhcp snooping trust'.

troubleshooting.symptom: Clients on a remote subnet never get an address even though the server is healthy.
troubleshooting.cause: No 'ip helper-address' configured on the router interface facing that subnet, so the broadcast never reaches the server.
troubleshooting.fix: Add 'ip helper-address [server-ip]' on the client-facing interface.

best_practices: Always exclude infrastructure/static addresses from the pool before defining it, to avoid double-assignment.
Keep DNS server and default-router options accurate in every pool.
security_notes: DHCP itself has no built-in protection against rogue servers — that protection is DHCP Snooping's job, not DHCP's.
interview_questions: Walk through the DORA process step by step.
Why is a DHCP Relay agent necessary when the server is on a different subnet?
What does 'ip helper-address' actually do to the broadcast packet?
exam_traps: Assuming DHCP alone provides security against rogue servers — it does not; that requires DHCP Snooping.
Forgetting that DHCPREQUEST is broadcast (not unicast) so other servers also learn their offer was declined.
enterprise_notes: DHCP Relay is essential in any enterprise where DHCP servers are centralized but clients are spread across many subnets/VLANs.
